questions.question: Explain the three levels of abstraction in software design: conceptual, logical, and physical. How does each level contribute to achieving modularity in large-scale systems?
keyPoints: Conceptual level: describes what the system does from user perspective (high-level business rules)
Logical level: describes data structures and relationships independent of physical implementation
Physical level: specifies actual hardware, software infrastructure and implementation details
Each level separates concerns, enabling independent development and modification of modules

questions.question: Differentiate between 'content coupling', 'common coupling', 'stamp coupling', and 'data coupling'. Among these, which represents the worst and best form of coupling, and why?
keyPoints: Content coupling: one module modifies another module's internal data (worst)
Common coupling: modules share global data structures
Stamp coupling: modules share composite data structures but use only parts of it
Data coupling: modules communicate only through parameters (best)

questions.question: A banking application has a 'TransactionProcessor' class that directly accesses database connection strings, performs validation, calculates interest rates, sends email notifications, and generates reports. Analyze how this design violates principles of abstraction and modularity. Suggest a refactored design applying SOLID principles that promotes independence and maintainability. Provide a class diagram showing the improved architecture.
keyPoints: Violates Single Responsibility Principle (SRP) - class has multiple reasons to change
Violates Dependency Inversion Principle - depends on concrete database/email implementations
Solution: Separate concerns into distinct classes (TransactionValidator, InterestCalculator, NotificationService, ReportGenerator)
Use Dependency Injection and Interface Segregation to achieve loose coupling and high cohesion

questions.question: In an e-commerce platform's order management subsystem, you observe the following design issues: (a) The Order class directly instantiates PaymentGateway, ShippingService, and InventorySystem classes; (b) Adding a new payment method requires modifying the Order class; (c) Changes in ShippingService affect Order class testing. Apply the concept of 'information hiding' and 'encapsulation of variation' to redesign this subsystem. Explain how your design achieves: (i) independent development of modules, (ii) easier testing through mocking, and (iii) runtime flexibility for adding new payment/shipping providers without code changes.
keyPoints: Apply Interface Segregation and Factory Pattern: create IPaymentProcessor, IShippingService interfaces
Use Dependency Injection to invert dependencies - Order receives dependencies via constructor/setters
Each module hides implementation details and exposes stable abstractions (Liskov Substitution Principle)
New providers can be added by implementing interfaces without modifying existing client code (Open/Closed Principle)
